speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Macy Groseclose Nye took place at her home in Indiana on March 11th, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Down the street there, they called him the sponsored school of healing. And he had old seven -ray doctors under him.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I've got the names of all of them down here, the doctors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the, what kind of, what does the foster school of healing, what did they do?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well I'll tell you what they had was a cancer cure. And that really worked. Because my mother lived to be 94 and when she was about 80, about the age of me, she had cancers come all over her face. And Dr. Foster healed every one of them. And that's what he was, was a cancer doctor. And this town had, oh you know, boarding houses, one on the corner there, one down the street, big one up there. And there was people from everywhere that came here.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Do you know an old fellow that's got a store at Pullman, a big store, named Bismore?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, his father had a store at Kamiah and he came here in the early days to be doctored, him and his wife. And that, the history of this town, having all these people here. Everybody's house had somebody boarding in it or staying. To be doctored. Yes sir.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do you remember about what kind of cure they had?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, he had this cancer cure. Of course, I don't think that he claimed to cure them after they got really, like you know, medicine doesn't cure them now after they get going. But when they was first come on, he could cure them.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: You never heard of him?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I heard of him, but I didn't know anything about what the Foster School of Medicine did. Well, they called the Foster School of Healing and he built that big concrete building down there. Nothing's in it much anymore. It used to be a store. And this town was a good sized town and they had all kinds of buildings that went to the depot down there on both sides of the street. And then this street that went this way and the one that went that way. But so many doctors. With all those doctors, they must have had an awful lot of people getting cured. Sure they did. You bet they did. This town was a big town in them days. People tell you that it wasn't so, but it did so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well besides cancer, were there cures for other illnesses too?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh yes, yes, yes. Dr. Foster treated all kinds of ailments. It didn't matter what and all these doctors that studied under him, he had hiss from the Palmer School of Healing someplace in the middle west. That's where he graduated from. And I'll tell you what made him leave here. This old E .W. Porter we was talking about. Well he had this little one horse bank down here and they moved it across the street over there. That was the first Methodist church we ever had. We had in that pool hall. And anyway he built him a new bank building on the corner. And well he was kind of crooked old Porter. He got to be a bank examiner so you know him. Well anyway he and Foster got into it, something other in the bank about him. A stealing foster bank, which he did, and they got in a fight and he knocked Foster down and broke his leg. And next week Foster left here he went to Clarkson and built him a beautiful building down there and had his school healing in Clarkson until he died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that take some of the life of the town with him right then?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: It ruined it. It ruined it. The businesses couldn't make it so they just had to fire and burn them. This whole side of this street from that old church there down there was all full of business buildings and they burnt every one of them. And they burnt all of this side too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well when you say they burnt you mean they just caught a fire?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: They just set it to fire. They just set him a fire. And of course this town didn't have much water. They had that big spring on the corner up there. My dad piped the town that little round reservoir but that wasn't a drop in the bucket you know what it was. Half the town burned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they know who set the fire?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Sure they did. They paid the insurance to him.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I could even tell you the names of them. Well you don't have to tell me. We can forget about the names but what happened is what is what's important. Well that's what happened to them. They had the three hotels. That old building that Chuck Nobles got his store in was the Palace Hotel. That's what it was built. And they had a brickyard down here by the railroad track and they made all these bricks. And this town made all these concrete blocks too that they built the Palace Hotel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When that fire came was it just one fire?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh no no no it was different times. Whenever the insurance got too heavy they set the fire. They just set the fire. And the town was built in wood and not in brick. Oh yes. It was all wooden buildings with generally a little you know porch out over and a little kind of a sun shade built out. They never got, now if I lived in town and I knew that somebody had set a fire and burned down part of my town I wouldn't be too happy about it. I'd probably want to you know do something back to them. Well I'll tell you about my father's business. He had a nice store down there and there was a dance hall between him and where Chuck Nobles this building is. Well two men had this dance hall that belonged to Howard Hutchison. And they had a piano in there and they insured it for fifteen dollars. And they set the fire and burned it and got the fifty dollars. And how I know that my dad had a great big old iron safe in his store. Well they saved the safe and this old woman lived down on Water Street. I forget what her name was. Well anyway she wrote a letter to Richie that was my dad's partner and said well we guess we got even with old growth folks in Midgerton. We burnt the place down. And she signed her name to it and they kept that letter in that safe.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well when they burnt them down they burnt the whole block. They burnt everything but Chuck and the reason they didn't burn it it was Rick Building and it was a hotel so it didn't catch the fire. And there was a great big apartment building on this corner. They burnt it. They burnt my dad's store. They burnt the whole block only that one building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to your dad then? Did he lose a lot of money from that?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: It just broke his heart. So then he moved his stuff in there and bought that old Rick Building that Chuck's got a store. He had a store in there. And then the upstairs he had his undertake business upstairs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he had a store too?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yes. And then my mother had a milliner shop in the front part of it. Oh. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well did was he able to did he lose a lot in there? Was he able to get anything from his insurance on the place?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh I don't think so. I don't think so. Boy that just must have been terribly rough. Oh it was awful. And you see this whole side over here all burned but that old Rick Building that's the Dr. Boring's build. They burnt everything and that Shable Hall that burnt on that corner was a big three story building. They lived in the bottom. They had a place where they walked out on the ground, the third floor you know. Then they had this big kind of old, I don't know what kind of a store it was. I don't remember but I think it was a furniture and paint. Then they had the odd fellas hall upstairs. Mm hmm. Oh yeah. This town really got set afire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well then at that point did they rebuild it in brick?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No they built that church over here in concrete blocks. No they never built another brick building in this town. I don't know what to come in the brick yard. It was down there where the warehouse sets now but then they never had another brick building. They had the concrete building, that old museum out there they built it out of concrete blocks. They built the church and they built this house. They built that house out there on the corner of Mikey Hendrohn's. And a little house down on State Street too, a little concrete block house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were talking about Foster a little while ago and I was wondering besides being the doctor did he take a very active part in the town besides that?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: My land he was so generous with his money. It didn't matter who wanted he give it. And he had this school of healing and he had lecturers come from other towns and then they would have these meetings once a week up there and lectures.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yes he was a really man that was a well business minded man and had an interest in the town. He really believed in it. That's how we come to come here. My father come from Kentucky to Spokane and he met Foster up there accidentally and he invited him to come here and so my father came here. No kidding. Yes. Oh that's really interesting. Yes that's how he comes to come here. That's how we found out about it. Well we had four trains today. We had plenty of ways to go where we wanted. It wasn't like today. Today you can't go any place. You have no way to go any place.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: So your father came and you came directly. From Kentucky. And came directly down here from Spokane. When he came out here then he didn't know for sure where he wanted to go. Oh no. Just knew he wanted to settle in here some place. No but Foster wanted him to come here because he wanted to build up this town and so my father went into this furniture business, this store business and then he bought out this R .H. Porter's Undertaken business and then they had the telephone in there too for all the rural out before Shoe for Heaven.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What had your father done back in Kentucky?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well that's another story. My grandfather was a millionaire and we lived in Kentucky. He had oil wells, the Atlantic and Pacific oil companies. But my mother's health failed her and he made up his mind that he would come west because it might help her. And it did. It cured her of everything she had and she did to be 94.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how old was she when she came west?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well I don't know. I don't know how old she was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But this was, was this the same, did he come out with your, with the, this was his, her father coming out with her?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well her father never come here. Oh her husband. Yeah. My father. Oh okay. Yeah. No my grandfather died in Kentucky. Uh huh. So he and your mother came out then. But my father had a lot of money from those days you see when he come here to invest. Well I'll tell you old Foster had a big interest in this town but when Porter knocked him down and broke his leg he moved and the town went to, you know, right now. Well sure a town, a little town, to have something like that there must have been the biggest thing in the world for it. Sure it was. To support it, bring in all that business. Yeah. I've got the names of all the doctors down and everything. I've kept track of it all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So with all those people there must have been a lot of people coming in from other, how far away did they come from? Would they come from as far away as Portland or Seattle?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh yes. They come from every place, South Idaho, everywhere. I can remember one old man Foster talking to him. I was a kid and stood around and listened to everything you know.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And he was telling him about his cancer on his lip and Foster said, well why didn't you come before you came?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: We could have cured it. He said, well I had the hay to put up. I had the hay to put up.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: See?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: He could have cured it if he had come when it first come there. But it had eaten away part of his lip the time he got here. Oh my father buried all them G -wits. You know I used to help him and I'd have night marriage till yet. Look at them poor fellas. My father didn't have too much education so when he had to get this license he had to take a course you know in all this. Well I did all his writing and learned it all. All the undertaking business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You did?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I was twelve.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To help him?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yes and I helped him too. And he buried the Indians and he got forty dollars from burying them and he had to furnish the casket and dig the grave and furnish the music and everything for forty dollars. And then he had to go over to the agency at Blackway to collect his money.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I always went along and phrased the music and did the singing and saved the organ. Had them old time organs you know. Oh when I was a kid I was afraid of the idea of dead body was in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You was?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well the idea just of course you know I never had death in my family when I was a kid. No no I was raised with it. You know when I got older I quit going to funerals. I don't care who's buried I don't go. I just got I don't know just too old to have anything to do with somebody. But when you were a kid you didn't find it scared you at the time. Oh no no it's just part of your life. It's just a part of you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how would you help your father with the bodies? Did you actually help him prepare them for a share?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Sure he always had what they called a cooling board. He had two of them. And usually they made him take them to the house and lay them out in the house. But he had a room in his house down here that he brought them home if they let him. You know and then he had these pro cars and these big bottles of stuff. And he drained all of the blood out and then he put the embalming fluid in and did body cavity work. You got fifty dollars for that if they had the fifty dollars. But you had no burial permits or anything so you didn't have to call the doctor and they could kill him and bury him if it's all right. Nobody cared if they did they couldn't do anything about it.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: But then he got his license in 1910. He had to take this course for his son's course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it kind of like it is now with the time between the death and the burial and the services? Was it pretty much the same?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yeah. Not the same. But they were less likely to use a parlor and more likely to leave the body at home and have them decked out at home. Some of them did. They wandered left at home so you left at home. But he when he moved in that old brick building down there he had several rooms up there for caskets you know on display. Then he had the rooms for lay them out you know and you could go there and see them. But lots of people wouldn't let him move them from their house. And sometimes you had the funeral in the house. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there were there many many ministers here to conduct services?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh yes this town had churches. They had a Catholic church, a Lutheran church, a Presbyterian church, a Christian church. Well I don't know how many. I've got them down in my history. Quite a few. Yes they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh and two there was lots of what do they call them?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: They've all quit here anymore. Well I can't think of it now. We had a preacher lived across the street here. One of them. You could hear him preach. He preached in the odd fellas hall down there and you could sit here and listen to him. He talked so loud you could hear him. His name was Hoskins.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh this town had plenty of preachers. Plenty of churches too.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And the German Lutherans had one up there on the corner and they preached in German and during the war they made them quit.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: We didn't have any sweets here but we had Germans.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think it was like for German people during the first war? Do you think they were kind of a little scared about what might happen?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I don't think they were scared but they sure wanted everything for their self. You know they wasn't for the United States in the war. They were for their self.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I guess they stopped teaching German in a lot of places. In schools they stopped teaching but you couldn't teach it in school. They had a German school at Cameron. They shut it. But I lived next door to an old German woman and I learned to talk German when I was little. Then when I went to school at Walla Walla I took German. Then when I taught school in the big bend I had to know it because that's all they ever talked over there.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: So it's a good thing I learned it wasn't it?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yes. I didn't know there were so many German families over there. Oh yeah. Russian Germans. Oh that's right. You see in the early days they gave every other section of land to remember to the government the homestead. Well the Russian Germans come and took it up.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: This town had lots of Germans. I think about this foster.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many people who died in town who weren't cured because they were too far gone?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh I think so. And then probably your father buried some of that too. Yes because these German houses as I remember they were full of patients and some of them were bent fast. Some of them were crippled you know. Some of them had different things than cancer. I wonder if there were many jobs then for the young women or older women to be nurses at the window hospital. No I don't remember any young women nurses. Now Foster's wife was a nurse and I'll tell you something about her and you can take it or leave it or leave it or think I'm a liar. They lived in that old B house up there and there was a window that opened onto the porch. They would come around saying and as long as that window was in that house you could stand out in the street or here and look up there and see her in that window. That's so. And the people that bought it green they moved there after Foster left and they had the window taken out and different class putty didn't do a bit of good. She stood right there.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: That's the truth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could most anybody in town see her?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: It got to be just commonplace. Nobody thought nothing about it. But Green stuck the glass out and put a new window and didn't do any good. I heard a story about, I never got it right but it was kind of garbled the way I heard it but it had to do with a baby getting killed and something to do with a nurse. Maybe the nurse was killing a baby or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that there?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No, I never heard that. Maybe that was in Kendrick. I never heard it. No, maybe it was. But nothing about a baby who died or got killed. No. Okay, it must be something else. What about, now Mrs. Eggers said that I had to ask you to tell me this story about the big fat man that hated you. Yeah, old John Garman. He homesteaded all that land that lays up there on this side of the hill, you know. At one time, long after he died, they had it all planted off and was going to build asylum there and then they didn't. But anyway, this old John Garman homesteaded all of that and this town had a lot of people that were spiritualists. You know, the Snyder's that they named the town after Juliaetta, their folks were all spiritualists.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what are spiritualists?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I've heard of that but I don't know what they know. Well, it's a kind of a religion, I guess. I don't know. But they believed in having seances and seeing all these things, you know. And so they had a big seance at one time and all of them sat together and the spirits told them to dig a coal mine over here on top of the hill, so they did. And they dug on it for years. They said it would be cold but there was never was no coal there. Never was. But this old Garman, he homesteaded there and the spiritualists, they got discouraged with themselves so he left here and he went to the Salmon River over on the Dumac someplace.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did other spiritualists go with him? Did a bunch of them leave?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No, no, they all lived here. But he was one of them. He was one of the main ones. And anyway, he had a young man that worked for him. I've got his name written down. And they went over there to live and he had lots of camp and lots of land, this old man, Norman. So he got sick in the summertime and he died. So he made this young man promise to bring him back to Juliaetta and bury him up on that hill so he could lay a curse on Juliaetta. So he died in the summertime in August and they hauled him out of that Salmon River country to Grangeville and got him a casket. Well, he swelled up to the big. They had to rope the casket around him and he brought him in a hack. And oh, he smelt for the time they got here. And they buried him up there in the field and piled a lot of rocks on him and put an old wooden cross there. And it stood there for years and years. And here a year or two ago, oh, it's been 10 years, I guess now, the Washington Water Power put a big pole right at the head of his grave. And I asked him to put his name on the pole, but I don't know if he did.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, I think Gussie told me that the pole went through what was left of his head. Oh, no, he did no such a thing. But I'll tell you, the people that farmed the place have moved the rocks and I think they farmed him. And there was an Indian graveyard there above the sandbank and they farmed it too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did he want to put a curse on Juliaetta?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, because he was discouraged with all these things that the spiritualists had decided Juliaetta would amount to and it never mounted nothing and the coal mine run out. And so he just washed his hands of it and put a curse on the laser and I guess it's on it too. Never mounted nothing since. Well, I'll tell you, Juliaetta is no different than a lot of small towns that got the same troubles in that way because it's just tough with the way the cities go. Well, that's the way Genesee has done. You know, Genesee used to be a big town. It had the railroad before Juliaetta ever did. It had lots of things, but it's gone through the doors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that when the Foster School moved, that was the end of the high times for Juliaetta?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yes, that's your life. That finished it. That finished Juliaetta. And he took all his doctors with him. They went to Clarkston too. Oh, it finished this place. And then everybody else had had a business here when Broke's was in Vernon Falls.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, my dad. He really thought about this place.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: He's very lucky Indians. And he got $40 for it too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to know any of the Indians around here?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, yes. I still know some of them, but you know, there's hardly any full blood left. Those Johnny Woods and just a few of them, they're all mixed anymore. We only have about one that lives here that Shores, Bob Shores lives up here on the hill. About the only Indians we've got. But the reservations down here are just a mile.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in the early days, didn't the Indians use Juliaetta a lot when they come into town here?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, I'll say they did, mile -teddy. We had the Indians for everything. And they went to our church too, the Indians.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: A lot of them were smart people, fine singers and everything, you know. They went to school up here too.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: We had lots of Indians.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, gosh. China money Indians, but we never had a nigger. The only nigger I ever remember coming here was one nigger George, and he lived down the canyon with the Indians. And he was a bronc rider. He won the money at Pendleton one year.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I don't think I've heard of him. That's interesting. I don't think I've heard of him before. But the first and only nigger we ever had that I remember of stayed here. And where I was born and raised in Virginia, we had 500 niggers there, a white person. But we didn't have any Juliaetta, and I was glad too. I was glad to get shut of all the niggers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you came out here from Virginia?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Seven. You came from Kentucky. Oh, from Kentucky, yes. I was seven. People ask me how I remember all this stuff. I remember all of us when I lived in Kentucky too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why can't you remember it?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I don't know why you can. I've known other, I've known some old timers that have good memories. Go back to the beginning. Well, sure, you can remember it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you was little, what's the difference?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: If you thought about it a little bit then, then I think maybe it helps. Well, yes, and I think I was that type of person that put everything down, everything. Even when I was little, I wrote everything down. Some people don't do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do you remember from Kentucky?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I'm curious.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember from before when you were even seven?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I'll get you a picture of the house we lived in. Oh, I'd like to see it. Then you'll know what I remember. I've got it here someplace. It was a great place.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And nowadays they try to teach them sounds, but they don't give them. But they're in these books.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I see. I think this looks like a good way to learn it to me. Well, it gives you a good foundation, something to go back to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The McGuffey's stories had good morals to them, too, didn't they?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yes. Well, they got good morals in that book. But this is an old McGuffey's spells book that I had. I don't know what grade I was in. I have no idea. I went to school in Kentucky, but in the South you didn't have public schools. You went to a private paid school. You wouldn't dare go to public school with the niggers, so they went, but you didn't. You would've got killed. I wouldn't have lasted a day. They were really dangerous. Well, little kids wouldn't have had no chance with a bunch of niggers. I never went to school only to a private school. And I was a little... I would come here, and here you could do your things in this country. You had your own school. You had your own church. You could go to anything, and you was free. Different set -up. I never wanted to go back to that country. And they've got it in that country. They've got it, I called it, cash system. You know, where if you're a lawyer or you're a preacher or something, you'd be different air than these other people down here.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: You wouldn't dare. Well, that's why my mother never washed or never did any work. She wasn't born to it. I learned how to work when I was little. So the idea was that the people who were the upper cross, they didn't associate. Well, they're still that way, too, in there, too. They're still that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they weren't supposed to associate with the common people?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No. They called them poor white trash.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: That was different. In this country, you was free. Everybody was the same. Here's according to what you knew, and if you had sense enough to make it, you could. And they're still that way in this country.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: That's interesting. I never thought about that. Of course, I never was in the South, but it sounds like a whole different kind of world. Yes, it is. I never wanted no part of it. But then it must have been kind of nice for your mother to have a rich father and not have to do anything. Oh, yes. She was a lady. You betcha. She was a lady. Well, I wasn't, and I was glad of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How big was this house again?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: It had four stories and 52 rules.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And it had a fireplace. There was a long hall like this on every floor. And the rooms opened into the hall. And every other room had a fireplace, and it had a little brick garden grate in there, and they burned cold in every one. That's kind of heat you had. It kind of sounds almost like a hotel, a fancy old hotel. Well, I guess you'd call it that. I don't know. That's the way it works.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I never liked that house.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: You know, I couldn't teach school. It wasn't because I wasn't a good schoolteacher. But I took home all the problems of my kids every night. And I couldn't sleep, and I cracked up under it. I couldn't, well, I couldn't leave my problems at the schoolhouse. I didn't like schoolteachers. And I, oh, I talked to retarded children too. You know, I majored in that. And that was a long thing for me. I didn't fit in that kind of work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, is that because the problems that the kids had really did get to you?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yeah, I took them home with me every night, and I couldn't do anything different. Well, maybe it just mattered too much to you. Yes, it did. Yes. I just couldn't teach school. I couldn't have been a supervisor or something, but I just quit. I'd rather farm.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: What kind of problems did the kids have that you found, you know, that... Well, most of them were too old for the grades they were in, you know. And they didn't have the power of reasoning. Maybe they'd have the mentality of a little five -year -old, and they'd be 14 years old. That was the problems you had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What could you do with a kid like that?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: He didn't have a chance to... Well, if you had the patience, you could teach him something, finally. But it took a long time. He would do the same thing over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come they would have gone for so long without ever learning anything?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, they didn't go to school, I guess. You know, they didn't have much opportunity back then. I think I graduated from Ellensburg in... well, 17, anyway, at the end of the war. The First World War, not the second one. Yeah, the first one.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: That sure is a nice book, isn't it?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Boy.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Do you sell these?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, the trouble with this one is that they sold out that whole issue all... Park. Park.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: 168 acres of timber.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Tim Ryan, if you would... 21.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yeah.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, that's right. Do you know... I was curious about how you came to... how you decided that you were going to teach. And then how you wound up getting the learning to do that to teach. Well, I went to Walla Walla School. I went to Columbia College two years. And then I come home and I wanted to make my own living, so I went through Lewis and State Normal. Then I went to Ellensburg State Normal. And then I taught. And you had to teach five years to get a life diploma. So I taught five years to get a life diploma in the state of Washington. And I could still teach in Idaho because I had my diploma from the Lewis and Normal two years.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: So that's how. Did they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: did they train you good for the classroom, or did you have to learn most of it when you got in the classroom?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No, no, no. You trained at Ellensburg. You took special lessons in all of it. And then you had to do your practice teaching in the city system over there, too.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: So you had it all right there in front of you.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: At Ellensburg was a fine school.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And one thing I liked about Ellensburg, now at Lewiston you went to the Normal. You wasn't any part of the town. You wasn't anything. You went to the Normal at Lewiston, at Ellensburg while you was a part of everything that went on in that town. And that's the difference in the two towns and the schools. I wonder why they were different. I don't know. I don't know. You went to the church in Ellensburg while you played the organ or the piano, whichever they asked you. And you was always in the choir. You was a part of the town. And we edited kind of a paper at school. And then if you had any business magic about you or anything, while you went around all these businesses and they gave you their business ads to put in the paper and they were interested in you. You were somebody. Oh, that Ellensburg, that was a fine school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where is this Columbia College?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, that was a mental school and it's been closed up for years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was it at?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: It was Milton, Oregon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was that kind of unusual?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I didn't think too many kids got to go to college in those days. I mean, they actually went to college.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do many kids around here going besides you?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No. Nobody went to me. They went to the normal quite a bit. You know, that was nice. But I had to get to the high school before I could go there. And this junior college had all that, you see, in two years of college.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: So that's how I got along so good in Washington. I had to go in Oregon. All three. It's true.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was going to that college like? Was that a place where you learned a lot?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, yes. It was a wonderful school. Yes, it was. They had dormitories, you know, and a big enrollment. And they had music and they had business courses. They had everything. All you had to do was have a little money to do it. And my brother called me up here a night before last and said my voice teacher at Columbia had died that night before and she was 95. Mrs. Thompson, her name was... So you had a lot of things that you could take up, you know. You didn't just have to, you could take business course or music course or anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you started teaching, where did you teach?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I taught the first year up on Pix Ridge at the Union Schoolhouse. And I'll tell you something and you can leave it or think it's alive, but I got $240 a month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A month?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yes. That's right. And I'll tell you why, because it was during the First World War. And when I talked down at Pomeroy, I had three... Well, there wasn't any teachers hardly. And I had three country schools in one. I had that many kids. That's the way it was out here. I had kids older than me. You had everybody who wanted to go to school. It didn't matter what district they come out of. Told you it wasn't any teachers and they did the best they could. Oh, my kids, they run away and got married in the middle of the school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find it hard to teach?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No. You found it easy. Yes. I never had any problem. Never had any problem with the kids or the curriculum or nothing. I never had any problem with them. Only when I told you I took it all home with me and I couldn't take it. That's interesting to me that you took it home with you. Every night I took every one of those kids' problems home with me. When you say you took the problems home, do you mean it was their personal problems or you wanted to be... Yes. I wanted to iron them all out and I wanted them to all be perfect and do everything just right. And they couldn't be. You couldn't do that. No help.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that you were strict in school, pretty strict with the kids?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, you know it. Well, when you had all kinds of kids from every district, big, little, old and young, you had to be strict. One winter I had 28 kids and 23 of them had the hook and cock and they went to school every day.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Now, that's something too. You had to know how to take care of them to keep them from bleeding to death with an old bleep, keep them from choking to death. I'll tell you, teaching was a problem in those days. And you did your own janitor work and carried in the woods, got the water, took your lunch, walked to school too. How many miles it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you boarding? Family?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. Yeah, you stayed someplace where you could.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Me and the schoolteacher. It wasn't no good. How long did you teach before you decided to... Well, I had to teach five years to get a life diploma. And that's how long you taught. Yeah, that's how long I taught.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I wasn't going to give up after I put in all that money and time was on to school, you know. But that life diploma was in Washington. It wasn't nothing to do with Idaho. But I had a teacher's certificate in Idaho. And I read in the paper where they're going to change everything now and they can't teach unless they have special degrees from the university.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that would make any better teachers?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Nope, but it'll make it harder for people to become teachers. And now they think they've got too many, so they're trying to make more hurdles in your way. Oh, for goodness sake. That's what I think it is. They keep raising the requirements. Yeah, yeah. They make it harder and harder. So they couldn't get a job. Well, I'll tell you one thing that I don't like about the school system. Maybe you do. Somebody gets a job at Juvie's house and he lives there and teaches till he dies. I don't think it's right. I don't think it's right. Maybe he's a good teacher. Maybe he's not. I think you ought to have a chance to hire a kick and fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Don't you?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: It makes sense to me, yes. If a teacher's not doing the job, you shouldn't have to keep it. They don't ever have to quit. Don't matter what they do or what they don't do. There they are. And they draw their money in the summer when they aren't even teaching.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, I'm just an old -timer, but I think that's the way it's wrong. I don't think you already inherit the school and keep it all your life, whether you're capable or not. Sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you about Juliaetta when in those earlier years that you were here, was it even after the town kind of changed around after the Foster School left, was there much life in town? I mean, did much go on? Who used the town? Countryside used it a lot?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, yes. Things were different because, well, they had Fourth of July and they had big parades and they had a band and they had a stand in militia and they had all this stuff and everybody came here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: It meant something. And the trains went back and forth so they could come and go someplace. It don't mean anything anymore. Just a wide place in the road. The faster you can go through it, the faster it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were some of these things like, like the Fourth of July? That was a really big affair?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh my, yes. And you had somebody to come, you know, some big speaker and they really made a fine speech. It was nothing to have the Governor.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I'll tell you, things have sure changed when it comes to math or something. You don't amount to anything anymore. I've got an old -time picture made in 1905 of the Fourth of July parade here and it's really something. It had a beautiful brass band and the militia and all these horseback riders and everything.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No, they don't have anything. The politicians don't even stop here anymore. Well, I saw President Tasker once. No kidding. Yes, sir. And I went down to the hotel one night to hear Madame Schumannheich sing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was she?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, she was a fine confounder, opera singer, you know, all over the world.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Things used to be something. Now you're nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember anything of Taft being here? Did he say anything when he got to town?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No, no. But my, he was an enormous big man and he had on a, I can remember he had on a great kind of an oil -cloth coat he looked to me like and had my mustache.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, I don't know. He didn't have anything on the map anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This contralto singer, was she, did she pack in the hall? Was there a lot of people to see?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: She didn't have anything at the hall. She just was at the hotel and we went to the hotel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she sing?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, yes. And she had everybody else sing, too. And she invited me to San Francisco to take lessons under her.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yeah. Old Juliaetta used to really be a place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What else went on besides Fourth of July and this, and people, famous people coming through? Were there other affairs?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: They had elections and the men was always the judges and the clerks. The women didn't ever work. I was the first woman to ever work on an election board in Juliaetta. All the men run elections and they had big old to -dos, you know, and the fireworks and all got drunk and shoot. Juliaetta used to have a brewery and a saloon and a winery.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I didn't know that. Yes, sir. They had a big brewery down here and they had a saloon down the other end of town and a winery out in that end and they raised lots of grapes, all kinds of grapes. What was the, I wonder if the wine was of good quality. Oh, yes, it was wonderful. A Frenchman by the name of Everleaf made it. Oh, it was good wine. Well, I've seen this whole town drunk at once. The whole town, everybody in it on wine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did that happen? What was the occasion?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Nothing. They just passed the wine around, I guess.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Big, them old and young was drunk.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: But, you know, we never told whiskey here for years and years and years and years. Still, that cafe down there took out a whiskey bottle. They had a local option here once when they salooned shut and they asked for a county right across the bridge so they just told the whiskey across the bridge.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And the first woman that I ever knew to run a saloon was here, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was that?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Her name was Rose Deeston. You must know Chris Deeston that lives up to Moscow out there on the highway to the other seas. No, I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that before prohibition or after?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, that was before I ever heard of prohibition. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she ran a saloon herself?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yes, she did. Rose Deeston. Well, that's kind of unusual for a woman to run a saloon. Yeah. Well, that was way back there when, too. I think it must have been 19 and 3 or 2. Long there someplace. I wonder if she did all right. Probably she had as good of a business as anybody else. Yes, she did. And nobody saw the thing about it, but the funniest thing. I was up at the fair one fall, a bunch of us old women, and this Rose Deeston's folks, there was one woman there. And they introduced me to her and she just turned around and just left just quick as a flash.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I thought, what the hell doesn't matter with you?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And she knew that I knew her mother.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, she was one of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was her mother that different from any other woman?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yeah, that's what I thought.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: What's the difference?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, she's afraid I'd tell all them, hupt, to hupt who her mother was. Well, I wasn't going to tell anything. I didn't care.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: At the time she had the saloon, I suppose she wasn't married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or was she?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yeah, she had Chris. He went to school here when I did. His name must be in that little pamphlet, Chris Deeston. But she ran it not her husband. He did. She didn't have any husband when I knew her. But I didn't think there was anything wrong with it. As far as I can see, she's a nice good woman. There was a... I guess that when local auction came in, local auction came in, it didn't really change things so much except that it wasn't sold openly. People were still drinking anyway. Oh, yes, everybody made it. Shoot.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: What was the difference?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: It wasn't that much different. No, no, there wasn't. Except I had a feeling from what some people told me that they had. People had to be more careful. Watch out because otherwise they could get pinched for it. Oh, yeah. Yeah. Yes, I guess they did.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I'll never forget that darn woman up there. She just flew out there when she found out who I was. Well, I got to care. I'll care who she was.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: There was a story that Mrs. Eggers told me to ask, make sure you told me. And that was a story about a girl who died, a young girl who died. She was out...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she out gathering honey or something like that?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, yeah. Jim Phelan's girl up this little creek here. Yeah, they had a big rock cliff and the bees had built their nets in there and honey just ran out of that cliff all down the side. And she was there getting honey and a rock fell off the top and hit her in the head and killed her. Jim Whalen's oldest girl was up around this little potlatch.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: She told me to make you tell me because she said you'd tell it the right way. Well, I don't know the right way or the left way, but I remember about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know when this Vassar died down here, did you read that in the paper, that Vincent? No, but was he one of the early Vassars from in this country?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, his father, Clyde Vassar, was one of the undertakers when my dad was here. Only he was the county coroner in this first county. Old Clyde Vassar. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that was pretty recent that he died, is the younger one?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yes, yeah, last week I think.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Used to be one of the undertakers of Pomeroy. I used to live at Pomeroy for years too. And one of the boys had undertaken part of over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when did you start your farming?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Nineteen and twenty.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that on the ridge?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yeah. I farmed up there for forty years. Farmed all over the country and moved seventeen times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, we rented different farms and different houses and some of the farms had houses and some didn't. You know, you moved.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of the farm life?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: You said you were looking. Oh, I loved it. I just loved farming. I was born to be a farmer and I farmed by myself for years. That's how I get the Social Security. I paid to the South. Nobody else did. I was left with one little boy, seven. And then one boy was in the, that was during World War II and one boy was in the Army, in the Air Corps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you farmed by itself, how much land were you farmed?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: When he left home, we had three hundred and sixty and it wasn't a cent paid on Northern nickel. So I just kept the land and the debt and paid it. Paid for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did you, what did you make, how did you make do as far as having to get all that work done, all the crops laid in?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, I had a boy about seven or eight and he worked and I worked. And I raised hogs to pay the taxes. You couldn't raise enough hogs, none of the paid taxes. They'd eat you out of the house and all you'd have to move away, give the whole thing to the hogs. Now that sounds, that is very interesting to me. I mean that sounds like a, like really a lot of work to do. I mean tough. Well, it sure does a lot of work. Especially if you had kids to raise at the same time. And this one boy, well I'll tell you something, you worked but I was a good worker. I raised a big garden too. We raised our own meat and cured it.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: We never was without plenty to eat.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And I'll tell you something else I did. I made me a house, put two old houses together, moved them, you know. And made me a house down on the highway where I lived with three quarters of a mile off and I couldn't get the REA. But if I moved me a house down there or had the dwelling there, the REA would build a line to me and I did. And they built me a house, a line to my house. So I had electricity.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far did you have to move then?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: All about a mile. Was an old homestead shack and was a good house. It was built when they didn't make the floor and put the thud on.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Women can't do the same kind of work that men can do. Oh, they can do anything if they're capable. And if they make their mind to it. Well, I'll tell you about me. I was always too independent for my own good. That's right. Well, I learned to work when I was growing up. My dad taught me to work. My mother never did anything you did at all at home. You went to school and made the cooking and washed the dishes. Your dad helped you. I never had any help from my mother because she was a lady.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: She was a lady, but she didn't mind if you worked. Oh, no. No, no. She went to San Francisco or Chicago or someplace every summer. She'd say, well, Macy can tend to everything. Macy will do it. Macy didn't.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And we had three boys at home beside me. I was the only girl. Well, then you must have had to do a lot if you had to do all the women's work. And my dad bought a car when I was 16, and I was the only one that drove it. I had to learn. And when I took my driver's license last fall up here, they asked how old you was when you got your first car to drive, and I wouldn't tell them. Otherwise, they wouldn't give me a driver's license.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And I'll tell you something about that driver's license. I've had one ever since they had it. I've never had a black mark on my driver's license. And that's something. I've never had it. I've, you know, been into it with the law, with the kids and all, but I never was with me. When you raise a bunch of boys, you're into it all the time.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: But, oh, I was passing my driver's license until, like, not when it was 80. Oh, shoot. I didn't go in a place much. I got tensed enough not to drive in town, you know. But I go a little bit. I go to the ranch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say that you were too independent, it got you into trouble, was that true? Could a woman be too independent in those days?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, I don't know, but I was. Well... It don't get you any love from your neighbors or your family or anybody. It gets you where you're going. If you make up for mine where you're going. Well, I think being independent is one of the very best things about a person to me.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, you do?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yes, I do. I think independence is the best thing in the world. My wife is very independent, and that's one of the things I like best about her. Well, that's good. Give me the men don't like the women to be independent. No. Well, I wouldn't have her any other way. Well, good.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: What does she do?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, it used to be. The reason that I say that... Well, you're not loved by your family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what I mean?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: You're too independent. You can do all these things, and, well, they feel like you don't need them to help you.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: You do need them, but then I don't know. I'm too independent, that's all. But if you weren't independent, you couldn't have farmed the farm by yourself. Oh, no. I wouldn't have had an acre of land.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And I hate to give it to the government, but what else am I going to do?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: The only thing you're exempt when you're old as me is $30 ,000 worth, and that wouldn't be nothing.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: When they value your land at $1 ,200 a acre, what would $30 ,000 worth be?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No, nothing if you got much land.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, isn't that awful?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I paid $40 an acre for that land. And to me, $40 is more than any $1 ,200 now. Oh, I lived my time out.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: You think so?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I don't know. Things have been changing fast, I think. Things have changed so much since you've been a grown person. Well, I heard the President make some kind of smart remark the other night about this people have done and leaving their land to their families. Well, they should be able to. Well, he never had any. It's always a matter of him. He never earned any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that in the early days, it was rough? It was really rough to get ahead enough that you didn't have to worry about having enough to make do?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I don't think you ever did. You think about now, some fella going up to Park to Homestead, a stone and timber claim, and he had to build him a house and live on it.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And how could you?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And build you a log cabin and live there, and the snow got deep in that damn house.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Huh?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, that's the way they got the land.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I don't know. I just think about that park and the people that I knew that lived there, and by the time they had, getting what they had.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And they had it, too. Them Tardisians, my, they had a hard life. You didn't have any telephone, you didn't have any radio, you didn't have any road, you didn't have nothing. Maybe two horses, and you couldn't raise enough to feed them, but they had big medas where Timothy grew, and you could cut some in the summer if you had them over, which you didn't have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Fix Ridge? Was it rough going Fix Ridge, or would it be easy compared to a place like Park?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, it was tough rule and pale. First year I was married, we had 60 other horses and didn't have enough to eat. Yeah, that's two. And we lived in a cook house. You had to take the wagon tongue up and not keep the rats from coming in.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, things were different. Well, that's just what, that's important to know, just what it was like then. I mean, I think that's really, that matters, that's the way it was. No, that's the way it was. That's the way it was. But I'll tell you something, that's homesteading that, that's Don and Timothy. And the people were playing, wasn't no song. If you did, you had to sing it yourself.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I want to have that one of Scott's twin, it's an innocent one. You'd take that one. I remember all about that park. And this, listen, it's such an interesting thing to have a prayer for you. Well, they had a little warehouse out there in the quick bottom, and it had a big windlass with big cables, and it went through up to the top of that hill. I can show you out there exactly where it went, by the little quick nastra to growing up and down that hill. And they had little cars, and one went up and pulled the other one, you know, and one got loaded and come down, pulled the other one up, and then they just put on this tight tweed. And they come down to this little warehouse and unload it and pull it back up. Yeah, I can show you exactly where it used to be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did most all the farmers, was it all the farmers on Potlatch Ridge that were using it?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No, no, just over there on the rim, I think, just over there. And the warehouse people was called Lawrence and Porter. That old Porter had the bank, E .W. Lawrence and Porter. But it worked. And I can remember, there used to be one of them tramways over there at Walla Walla that come off of a Mayview clear down into the State River Canyon. One of them tramways.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember it?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I heard that there were some over there. I didn't know that one in particular. But that makes sense. That's pretty steep canyon in there. You bet your life. That's lots worse than this little one they had here. Yeah. But they used it, I guess, for several years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about that Adams fella who started it, who built the castle? What do you remember about him?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, I remember him just as well as yesterday. And, oh, he was kind of a, I don't know, he had several children. He had Ben and Edmund and Meeta, one that married E .W. Porter. He had several children. But his wife died before he came here, and an old man named Grandpa Stevens came with him, and he raised the children. And then this old man built this, he called it the castle when he built it. He built it out of these concrete blocks, and they made them the hand. But you see the difference in my house and that castle house, this is slaps and plastered, and that damn thing, it just finished up on the inside of the blocks.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: But that old man, he was a card.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: His daughter, oldest daughter, Meeta, married a fella, and they had one child. And then old man Adams got this thing in his mind. I forget the name of the man that brought him the wheat that had all the heads on it, you know. And he sold that, and he sold that seed to the government, to the post office. And so the government overhauled him. And this son of all shot himself to keep him going to the pen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was he going to go to the pen?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Because they had misrepresented their seed on the post office of government.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: See?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, he made lots of money out of that wheat. It wasn't sold. It wasn't nothing. A word of his soul.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he claim for the wheat?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, he claimed it had seven heads and all this stuff, and it only had one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did Adams, did he discover this wheat, or did somebody discover it?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, yes. He found it in old Tuck Ackerman's tomb or someplace in Egypt. Shoot. Big bye.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: He was a crook, old man Adams.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder, did he get by with this for a very long time before they caught up with it?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, yeah. One summer, and they planted a crop and found out it wasn't sold, and then the government picked him up. Well, Mr. Noble told me that he was pushing it off as hard wheat and it was soft wheat. Well, that didn't have nothing to do with it. He said it had seven heads, and it would produce all this stuff which it didn't. It didn't produce nothing no more than any other wheat. I don't know.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: But this son -in -law shot itself, so he wouldn't have to go to Penn.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Then old man Adams took a notion he'd go to Florida, and he'd come here to visit my mother, and all they talked and cried over each other. Oh, my mother was one of these, well, I don't know what kind of a... Well, anyway, she built the churches and she built the houses and she'd give the parks, and you know that kind of woman. So they cried over each other, and he went off to Florida, and a streetcar ran over him and killed him, I think. They brought him back here in Burley, and he's buried out in the cemetery. His name was Abraham Adams. Your mother then was a real social center of this town. Oh, you know it. I'll say she was. She converted the whole country. And she and old working French started the Spalding Park.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yes, sir, they did. Now they give it back to the Indians. And you can't go there. All the signs is, you can't park, you can't park, you can't park. And they took the bridge out, you have to go to Lewis and come back to get to it. Shoot. Yeah, she and old working French started that park. Sent out trees from their place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say she was converting the country, do you mean the Indians mostly, or...?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No, no, all the white people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did she convert them to?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Religion. She was a great Methodist. Oh, yeah, she was a tough woman.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: She didn't have time to do anything at home. She's kind of like Miss Earl Clyde. She, you know, she's kind of like that. She's always got her fingers in something, something. She come down here to Spalding one night and sent me word to come. And I said her word, I wasn't able. She said word back to the woods, but it wouldn't do it. Well, I didn't want to go. Shoot.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, that's the way my mother was. She was always something, doing something for the community. Starting a church, her doing something. She didn't have time for home. I never learned how to keep house. She never knew. I never knew. I don't know how to keep house. I just live here.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: But old man Adams, he was a promoter, if you know what I mean. Yes, I know just what you mean. I've heard that word before. Yeah, that's what he was. He promoted himself on the wrong side, that round. Well, it seemed like a pretty bad deal. I mean, you'd have to figure you'd get caught in something like that. Well, of course you would. If you'd have good sense. But I guess his mind was in the clouds.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: But old Grandpa Stevens raised all his kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Raised Adams' kids?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they related somehow?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, he was Adams' wife's father. I think they'd come here from Minnesota.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much of a town government then? I mean, did they have a mayor and all that business?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Sure, but that was a mayor. Sure, they did. And they also had a lawyer, and they had a water commissioner, they had a street commissioner, they had a cop that put him in jail, and they had a jail. I see. That's the same jail. Oh, no. No, you never saw this jail. It was down by the warehouse, and it was built out of logs, and it didn't have a window. It had one door. Boy, and they put him in there. They put him in there. They don't have any jail now at all. Juliaetta hasn't any law. That cop can't arrest anybody. He couldn't tell you nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that something?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, in those days, there was people put in jail. You bet your life they put him in jail, and they left him there, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd take local people?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I mean, people could tell you. Yeah, I got there once.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What'd you do to deserve it?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: All I did was deserve it, but I got there anyway. Anyway, I think I did, but I got in there.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I can't imagine they kept you for very long. No, they was glad to get rid of me.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: But they had a really jail. This thing they had down here years ago, they couldn't arrest people and put them in there. It was a little kind of an iron thing, you know, in the corner. But they took it out and made a beautiful office there with red carpet on the floor and a big old safe that was in my dad's store. They got everything in there. And a town clerk, I asked him if he got paid, and he didn't answer me. I bet he didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was drinking the usual cause something would get thrown in jail?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, I think so. I think so. That's what I would imagine. But there used to be lots of fighting and shooting going on, you know, lots of it. And this town had a bunch of horse thieves, my god, and cattle thieves, and they sent them to the pen, and one woman was in it too, Ed Beard's sister, Bertha. And her husband, old Ed Grant, he served her term and his term, and when he came out of the pen, she was married to somebody else. Oh, boy, we used to have horse thieves from here to date in Washington.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, whose sources would they steal? Would they steal any farmers around here?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yeah, it didn't matter. They had a long rope. And cows, why, gee whiz, they'd steal your neighbor's milk cow and take it down to the bushes and butchers. Tell it to the butcher shop. Judy and I had two butcher shops.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did everybody know who they were?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Sure, they did. But when they picked them up, when they, the reason they got them, they picked up old Frank White and he turned state's evidence, he turned them all in, and they turned him loose. And there's one kid that looked up,

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: and he was 18, and they took him to the pen too.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, boy, we had a bunch of horse thieves and cow thieves. I've heard people whose stock got stolen. Oh, you betcha.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how dangerous was it? I mean, was it the kind of thing that probably wouldn't happen to the average farmer, or would he be lucky if they didn't get some of his stock stolen? I mean, how often would it be somebody would have something stolen?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, if you had anything, I'd get it stolen any night. They picked them up there from Dayton here. Mostly horses in that Dayton country, mostly cattle in this country, cause they'd bond to the Indians. It'd be probably easier to pick off the Indians' stock. Yeah, you betcha. They stole all the Indians' stuff. And the Indians' horses too.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, boy, we really had a bunch of cattle and horse thieves here once. And they were mostly local. I mean, the thieves were too. Yeah, sure they were. They lived right here. I'm surprised they didn't get vigilantes out after them. I mean, the farmers, you know. They'd be known to take that kind of action when things get rough. No, they didn't. They didn't say a word. They just let them steal them.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, boy, everybody knew who was doing it. That's usually the way it is. You can't do something like that in this place. No, everybody knew it. I'm surprised that the community couldn't take care of that better, you know, stop it somehow. You know, that's been so long ago. I forgot who the sheriff was.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I don't know. I never even thought of it before, no wonder.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: But, you know, years ago, they had, I don't know whether you'd call them FBI or what, but you had government men that picked things up like that instead of the sheriff. There was some government men picked all these up. And the government picked up old Adams. It wasn't the sheriff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think it was the FBI?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I'll bet it was. It depends on how long ago it was, but if it involved the Indians, then probably the federal government could get mixed up in it pretty easily, too. Well, I don't know. I hadn't thought about it. I do remember this, though. Something about Pinkerton. Oh, yes. Well, they're a private agency, but they work for the government a lot. Well, anyway, that had something to do with it.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, they sent plenty of them to the pen.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And that boy was 18. He served a year and a half and cost old Billy Cox $3 ,000 in them days. That was his stepfather.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: $3 ,000 is a fine?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Mm -hmm. Cool. Boy. No, he stayed down there a year and a half.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I was going to ask you a little bit, too, before I go about the depression, if that had much of an effect around here, you know, on the depression. Oh, yes. My goodness. I'll say it did. People didn't have a thing in the world on what they raised. And, well, I don't know. It wasn't so hard on people because they didn't need any money. They had everything in the world to eat at home. You didn't have any clothes, you know. The kids didn't have any clothes either. So it was all alike.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Boy, that was something. But you can't tell anybody about it now because they don't know what you're talking about. They have no idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See what I mean? Well, you mean these younger people that never lived through it?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yeah. They don't know what you're talking about. You can't tell them anything. They don't know. They've had everything in the world. You've had everything in your life that it is for you to have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could you get an idea of doing without everything?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, I certainly believe that it was a terrible time. I mean, people have told me that, and I just imagine that it had to have been. But, no, I can't experience it myself unless it happens to me. Well, you didn't have a thing in the world. Asterix, let's see who it is.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yes, it's Macy's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want to speak to her?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, no, that's okay. Why don't I... I'll put her on. Say hello.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: She's happy to help you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hello?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No, I haven't.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, all right. Oh, it's my neighbor right over there. She said, well, if she's got company, I'd...

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, when we have company, we don't...

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I couldn't even hardly know what it was. No, you couldn't. You wouldn't have any idea.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Only thing about it, if you had a garden and you had some hulks or something at home, you had plenty to eat, but you had no money. You had no money, and it hit the farmer so bad because they couldn't pay the taxes, and they took their land that they had homesteaded and bent on all their lives. You know, they just... You couldn't pay your taxes. You didn't have a cent. There was no money. Wheat was too big.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: You had no clothes at all, but thank the Lord you had plenty to eat.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: You know what hurt me worse than the depression was old Hoover during the war and doing about some meat.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: That hurt me worse than depression. No sugar, no flour, no nothing.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Old me of bread.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Was that all...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was all kind of a quota?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: You just gave so much a... Yeah, that was worse.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No sugar, no flour, no nothing.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Do you want that Juliaetta of work or not?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, now I'll tell you, it's a priceless treasure. Well, listen, if you... On the weather report, it makes its own weather.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: And the nearest town that we're to, the same weather is Walla Walla. We're not like Lewiston, we're not like Spokane or nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean what the weather is here is going to be pretty much the same in Walla Walla probably?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Yeah, I used to live down there and go to school there. And then I always keep track of the weather, you know, all places in Walla Walla and that's just so... I wonder why it's different here than... I don't know. Why, Kendrick and I said, never the same.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, really?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No, that place is a lot colder than the winter and a lot hotter than the summer. You can go up there and there'll be a snowman like hell come down here in the sun. There ain't no snow. This town makes its own weather. I think that old man, John Norman, had something to do with it.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, shoot.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: He sure put a curse on this town, didn't he?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, I don't know. I think it's a nice town. I like it. Well, I liked it like it was until they got to digging it up and putting the jewelry. I don't like it. Did you seriously consider wanting to move elsewhere besides Juliaetta in these...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, since you've been adult, have you always just wanted to live here?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: No, no. I never wanted to live here. I come here to take care of my mother. And when I got older, it was here in that state. And they were even moving magicians. They were moving up. I'd like to rather live on the hill. But you can't. You're just in the way so you just live here. Make your own life. I didn't want to live here. I hate this old house.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I'll tell you. I wish I had such a nice house to hate. Looks like you got a nice place to live.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, I don't keep it. I just live here. I've heard sweat sometimes. When I find a boy or a girl, they'll do it. Some of them are pretty good, too. Some of them are sweet to poor achievement. Once they've swept them all.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, God. No, I'd rather mess it like it was. I don't want to dig it up and put it in the sewer system.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Tumble of it is it won't work when they get it dug up. Haven't got enough water to run it. You know, people use so darn much water, they haven't got a lick of sense. Not a lick. Ain't no sense, you know.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Well, you think in the early days, they had to be a lot more careful. You betcha. All the water I used to have at the ranchers was what I carried there. You know.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: I don't think it's right. Just turn on the faucets and turn on the outside water in the summertime. This darn manor down here, this thing that they built for the old people, they turn on the water down there in the summertime and I can't get a drink up here. All the water runs out down there and it just runs down the path. Just runs any place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far did you have to haul the water on the ranch?

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: Oh, man. I lived one place and I had to haul it three quarters of a mile. That's where I moved my house to where the water was.

speaker: Maeci Groseclose Nye
words: We always had good water. And nowadays they put too much crap in the water, you can't drink it. Well, I could turn on the faucet and smell that damn water.
